title: Operating at fleet scale — process and systems maturity
subtitle: Multi-agent coordination, state that crosses boundaries, and the mechanics that bite at scale
aigp_intro: This act trains the governance of the build system itself: what happens when one builder becomes several concurrent agents sharing a machine, a repository, an event loop, and a memory ceiling. Its lessons pair the IAPP AIGP Body of Knowledge's Domain I competencies — establishing and communicating organizational expectations (I.B) and policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle (I.C) — with Domain III's governance of designing-and-building and of release, monitoring and maintenance (III.A, III.C). The cross-cutting anchor is the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's GOVERN function: accountability structures, processes, and risk culture that hold under scale. ISO/IEC 42001's plan-do-check-act management-system pattern recurs wherever an observed process failure fed a structural redesign of the process — this act's signature move.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development

lessons.title: Two Claudes on one machine share one HEAD.
lessons.failure: The model-sharing build went out as a second Claude session in a separate window, treated as fully independent. It was not: both sessions shared one physical working tree, and therefore one HEAD — git's pointer to whichever branch a checkout currently sits on. While the successor's Phase 1 commit was finishing on its feature branch, the predecessor ran `git checkout main` on the shared tree to commit a journal entry, and the successor's commit landed on `main` during the roughly thirty-second window that checkout opened. The tell was a benchmark partially failing: the second run launched after the branch flipped back, and the on-disk script no longer carried the flag the first run had used. Recovery traced the reflog and merged `main` into the feature branch — non-destructive, and cleaner than cherry-picking, because the same commit reaches both branches.
evidence: 2026-05-22 — Sharing the 14B (the second me, picking up the brief)
2026-05-22 — The instrument, and a hand-off to a second me
lessons.lesson: Two sessions can share a hard disk; they cannot share a checkout. Whenever a second agent must commit on the same repository, give it its own working directory with `git worktree add` — the fix is isolation, not 'be careful.' The companion rule: the predecessor stays off the GPU while the successor benchmarks, because a busy machine poisons the data the other session is paying for.
lessons.growth: The failure happened because the isolation you perceived (two windows, two conversations) lived at a different layer than the coupling that mattered (one filesystem, one HEAD). Concurrency bugs are almost never visible at the layer where you think about the actors; they live in the shared mutable state underneath them. Before you parallelize anything — agents, scripts, teammates, CI jobs — enumerate what they share: a checkout, a port, a database file, a cache directory, a GPU. For each shared thing, either give every actor an isolated copy or accept explicit serialization. And notice how the failure announced itself: not at the collision, but later, as a confusing downstream symptom — a benchmark missing its flag. When a tool misbehaves inexplicably during parallel work, ask what invisible state another actor may have flipped under you.
lessons.growth_question: Two sessions in two windows look fully independent — what single piece of state did they still share, and at which layer does parallel work actually collide?
lessons.diagram:
sequenceDiagram participant S as "Successor session" participant T as "Shared tree (one HEAD)" participant P as "Predecessor session" S->>T: Phase 1 commit underway
on the feature branch P->>T: git checkout main
(thirty-second window) Note over T: successor's commit
lands on main P->>T: journal entry committed,
switch back S->>T: benchmark run 2 launches Note over S: on-disk script has lost
the first run's flag Note over T: recovery: reflog, then
merge main into feature
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain I.C — establish policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle: a multi-agent development operation needs written procedures that isolate shared infrastructure, because vigilance does not scale across actors. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's GOVERN function — the cross-cutting home of processes, roles and accountability structures — where the worktree rule became standing procedure rather than a habit held by one agent.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Parallel actors collide in shared state, not in plain view; isolate the substrate by standing procedure, not by carefulness.

lessons.title: Continuity of intent across agents is a file-system property, not a memory property.
lessons.failure: The journal discipline that compounds the operator's portfolio — failures kept in, trade-offs named, lessons distilled into a numbered list — had been paid for by the Layer 3 over-build and a near-quit, and it worked exactly as long as it lived in the head of whichever agent was carrying the work. The moment a fresh session opened without it, it stopped working. When the operator asked whether the agents to come would automatically write entries in the shape his IAPP AIGP certification track needs, the honest answer was that nothing guaranteed it. So the discipline was codified as a CLAUDE.md section — 'Build-Journal Discipline (NON-NEGOTIABLE)' — that every session reads at startup: when an entry is required, the form, the content, the anti-patterns. The entry called the codification 'the apology for assuming continuity that the file system did not actually provide.'
evidence: 2026-06-02 — Making the journal discipline standing instruction
lessons.lesson: If you want the next session to do the thing this one learned, write it down where the next session will read it — not where you will remember it. Standing instructions belong in the file every agent inherits at startup; the journal entry beneath them is the evidence the instruction earned its standing. A rule held in one place should be held in two.
lessons.growth: The mechanism is turnover: every fresh agent — and every new hire, contractor, or future-you — starts from the written substrate, not from anyone's memory, so a rule that exists only in a head has a lifetime of exactly one session. You can recognize the gap whenever you hear 'we always do X' about something written nowhere an inheritor actually reads. The prevention is to identify the startup surface in any project — the onboarding document, the configuration file, the checklist that genuinely gets read first — and put standing rules there, then treat every repeated verbal correction as a signal that a written rule is missing. Culture you cannot point to in a file is culture that resets to zero at the next handoff.
lessons.growth_question: If every rule your project relies on lived only in the head of whoever worked yesterday, what survives the first morning they are not in the room?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["journal discipline held in
the working agent's head"] --> B["session ends"] B --> C["fresh session opens
without it"] C --> D["rule lifetime:
exactly one session"] E["codified: CLAUDE.md
Build-Journal Discipline"] --> F["every session reads
CLAUDE.md at startup"] F --> G["rule inherited before
any product code"] D -.-> E
lessons.aigp_text: This is AIGP Domain I.B — establish and communicate organizational expectations for AI governance: an expectation is established only when it reaches every actor by default, in writing. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's GOVERN function, which locates risk culture and policies as the enabling foundation, and ISO/IEC 42001, whose artificial-intelligence management system (AIMS) rests on documented expectations rather than individual memory.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Expectations bind only when documented where every actor reads them by default; memory does not carry governance across handoffs.

lessons.title: A setting that must cross a privilege boundary has to travel by a channel that crosses it.
lessons.failure: Wiring the new native window into the launcher, the obvious switch was an environment variable — `BLARAI_UI=winui`. But the launcher self-elevates: it relaunches itself through a UAC (User Account Control) prompt to gain the Administrator rights Hyper-V needs, and a Windows elevation relaunch begins with a fresh environment. A variable set in the un-elevated shell simply does not exist in the elevated process, so the selection would have silently fallen back to the terminal. Worse, no test could ever catch it: the elevated, GPU-bound path cannot run on a headless machine, so every available check would pass while the real path quietly did the wrong thing — only the operator at the live screen would see it. Reading `request_elevation` instead of trusting a mental model showed it forwards `sys.argv` but not the environment; the choice became a `--winui` command-line flag, which rides across.
evidence: 2026-06-02 — Phase 3: a real model behind the window, and a flag that survives elevation
2026-06-02 — The same privilege boundary, the other thing that doesn't cross it
lessons.lesson: When a value must survive a trust or process boundary — UAC, sudo, a container edge, a service account — it has to travel by a channel defined to cross that boundary, not one that merely happens to work on the same side of it. Read the crossing code to learn what is actually forwarded; do not trust the mental model.
lessons.growth: Boundaries drop ambient state on purpose — that is their security function: a fresh environment, a default working directory, a clean import context are how an elevated process avoids inheriting an attacker's influence. Your code, though, leans on ambient state constantly, which is why this same boundary bit the project three more times in other costumes — package imports, drag-and-drop, file dialogs. The portable skill: whenever your value must cross an elevation, a sandbox, a container, or a remote hop, list what the boundary's contract explicitly forwards (here, the argument list) versus what it deliberately discards (the environment), and route through the forwarded channel. Then make the crossing fail loudly when the value is absent — the silent fallback was what made this bug invisible to every test.
lessons.growth_question: An elevated relaunch starts the new process deliberately clean — of everything your code relies on, what is explicitly forwarded across, and what silently vanishes?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["un-elevated shell sets
BLARAI_UI=winui"] --> B["UAC relaunch
(launcher self-elevates)"] B --> C["environment: dropped
(fresh start by design)"] B --> D["sys.argv: forwarded"] C --> E["variable gone - silent
fallback to the terminal"] E --> F["no runnable test can see
the elevated GPU path"] D --> G["fix: carry the choice
as a --winui flag"]
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain III.A — govern the designing and building of the AI model and system: how configuration crosses privilege boundaries is a design decision with security consequences. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's secure-and-resilient trustworthy characteristic, and, at concept level, the EU AI Act's cybersecurity expectation for high-risk systems — controls must hold across exactly the trust edges they are built upon.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
aigp_frameworks: NIST AI RMF — secure and resilient (trustworthy characteristic)
EU AI Act — cybersecurity obligation (concept level)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Values that must cross a privilege boundary ride channels defined to cross it; ambient state is dropped by design.

lessons.title: One shared event loop makes any single blocking call a whole-app freeze — fix it structurally (off the loop), not locally (make the blocker faster).
lessons.failure: The backend serves every request through one event loop — the single scheduler all asynchronous work shares — via `loop.run_until_complete(...)` per connection. The voice methods knew this: transcribe and synthesize push their blocking model calls onto worker threads with `asyncio.to_thread`, leaving the loop free. The document methods three functions above them in the same file did not: `_m_load_document` called the blocking loader directly on the loop, and `_m_store_attachment` did it twice. So the moment a photo attach ran its eager vision pass, there was no loop left to service the microphone frame that arrived a minute later. The live boot put numbers on it: a voice clip captured at 22:10:39 was not handed to the transcriber until 22:15:27, then transcribed in under a second. The five-minute voice queue was not slowness — it was a thread starved of its turn.
evidence: 2026-06-04 — Off the one lane: the freeze was structural, so the fix had to be
2026-06-04 — The slowdown the live boot found, and the log that said it wasn't mine
lessons.lesson: When work is slow on a single-loop — or single-threaded, single-lane — system, ask what is holding the lane before asking how to speed the work. Fix it structurally, moving every blocking call off the loop, not locally by making one blocker faster. Then pin the property with a test asserting the work ran on a different thread than the loop, so regression announces itself.
lessons.growth: The mechanism is contention on a shared serialized resource: one loop means any single blocking occupant freezes everything behind it, and per-component tests structurally cannot feel it because the contention is the bug. The tell worth training your eye on is asymmetry — the same discipline applied to one set of handlers and not to the set beside them, the gap that opens when a pattern lives in one author's head instead of where the next feature inherits it. In any future system, identify the shared lanes (an event loop, a UI thread, a single worker queue) and make 'nothing blocks the lane' an explicit, written, test-locked invariant. And prefer locking the real invariant — thread identity — over a timing proxy that flakes under load.
lessons.growth_question: When everything queues behind one slow operation, is the operation too slow — or is it standing in the only lane the whole application has?
lessons.diagram:
sequenceDiagram participant D as "Document methods" participant L as "The one event loop" participant V as "Voice path" Note over V: voice methods already use
asyncio.to_thread D->>L: _m_store_attachment runs
blocking work ON the loop Note over L: eager vision pass
holds the only lane V->>L: mic frame at 22:10:39 Note over L: no loop free
to service it L->>V: handed to transcriber
at 22:15:27 Note over V: transcribed in
under a second
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain III.A — govern the designing and building of the AI system: availability under concurrency is an architectural property, not a tuning knob. The structural fix plus a regression test on the off-loop invariant exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's valid-and-reliable trustworthy characteristic and its MEASURE function — home of TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) — verifying the system meets its concurrency contract rather than observing the symptom fade.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
aigp_frameworks: NIST AI RMF — valid and reliable (trustworthy characteristic)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: One blocking call on a shared loop freezes the app; fix structurally off the loop and test-lock the invariant.

lessons.title: A deferred resource risk comes due as a hard failure, not a graceful one — and a fail-closed default can disguise it as another subsystem's bug.
lessons.failure: The vision MVP cached its ~5 GB model in a module global and never freed it. On the integrated Arc 140V GPU there is no separate video memory — that 5 GB lived in the same 32 GB system RAM as the always-resident 14B (~8.7 GB) — so over a multimodal session memory saturated and the host froze at 100%. The risk had been deferred in the design record as the 'open hard problem' — deferred without being bounded. And it wore a disguise: under memory pressure a vision turn exceeded the 120-second IPC (inter-process communication) timeout, the gateway's fail-closed default denied, and the user saw a 'validation error' — naming the policy validator for what was resource exhaustion three layers away. The log proved the disguise: the orchestrator was still working 95 seconds after the gateway gave up, ingesting at 08:28:34 a turn already denied at 08:26:53.
evidence: 2026-06-04 — The deferred problem came due, wearing another bug's face
lessons.lesson: Free what you cache on a shared, finite resource — an unfreed cache is a slow leak that ends in a freeze, not a warning — and never defer a resource risk without bounding it. Separately, make a fail-closed control report why it closed: 'denied by policy' and 'denied because nothing answered in time' are different failures with different owners.
lessons.growth: Two mechanisms compounded here. First, resource exhaustion degrades nonlinearly — fine, fine, swap-thrash, frozen — so a deferred memory risk does not ramp politely toward you; it arrives as a cliff. Any time you write 'we'll deal with the resource cost later,' attach a bound the same day: an eviction, a cap, an alarm. Second, safety defaults that collapse failure causes manufacture false diagnoses: the deny was correct, but its surface message pointed at the wrong subsystem, and you can lose days debugging the scapegoat. Audit your own fail-closed paths for whether they can say why they closed, and when debugging, trust the layer that names causes — the log distinguished 'default deny (no result)' from a policy denial — over any surface that collapses them.
lessons.growth_question: When the screen says 'validation error,' how would you know whether policy refused the answer — or whether nothing answered before the door closed?
lessons.diagram:
sequenceDiagram participant U as "User's screen" participant G as "Gateway (fail-closed)" participant O as "Orchestrator" Note over O: VLM ~5 GB cached forever -
RAM saturates, swap-thrash G->>O: vision turn Note over G: waits 120 s
for the first frame G->>U: timeout - default deny,
shown as 'validation error' O->>O: still ingesting
at 08:28:34 Note over G: turn already denied
at 08:26:53
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain III.C — govern the release, monitoring and maintenance of the model and system: a risk accepted at design time must carry a bound and must surface diagnosably in operation. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MANAGE function — residual risks are tracked and monitored, not parked — and, at concept level, the EU AI Act's record-keeping and logging obligation: the log, not the user-facing surface, held the truth.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
aigp_frameworks: EU AI Act — record-keeping and logging (concept level)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Bound every deferred resource risk; make fail-closed controls name their reason, or they misdirect the response to a scapegoat.

lessons.title: Verify the merge landed where you meant it to — a shell's working directory can drift, and a loop's "OK" echo is not proof.
lessons.failure: The decision-free half of the Tier-1 security wave ran as five specialist agents in parallel, each in its own git worktree (a separate working folder sharing one repository), each branch carrying one audit finding. The merge loop printed 'OK' five times, the commit graph looked clean, and `main` appeared to advance to the integrated commit. It had not: the shell's working directory had silently drifted into one of the agents' worktrees, so all five merges landed on that worktree's branch while `main` never moved. The verify-on-disk habit caught it before a single test ran — grep the merged tree for each of the five control markers (all present), then `git rev-parse HEAD` in the main repository by absolute path, which still sat at the pre-wave commit. The integrated commit was a clean descendant of `main`, so recovery was a fast-forward, not a rewrite.
evidence: 2026-06-04 — Five locks in parallel, and the merge that landed on the wrong branch
commit 6994b49
lessons.lesson: A command that prints success proves the operation ran somewhere — not that it ran where you intended. Confirm the branch, the HEAD, and the working directory by absolute path before trusting any multi-step git sequence, and never trust the loop's own success echo over the repository's own answer. This is the test-as-oracle trap wearing version-control clothes.
lessons.growth: The mechanism: the echo and the intent verify different propositions. 'OK' meant each merge succeeded relative to wherever the shell happened to stand — a moving frame of reference you did not know had moved. Any scripted sequence in a stateful environment can succeed against the wrong target: the wrong directory, the wrong branch, the wrong cluster, the wrong account. The discipline that generalizes is postcondition checking from ground truth: after the operation, read back the one fact you actually care about — did MAIN advance? — by absolute reference, from the system of record, not from the tool's progress output. Build that read-back into the loop itself, so the verification cannot be skipped on the day you are tired.
lessons.growth_question: Your merge loop printed 'OK' five times — what exactly did each 'OK' prove, and relative to which working directory was it true?
lessons.diagram:
sequenceDiagram participant L as "Merge loop (shell)" participant W as "An agent's worktree branch" participant M as "main (absolute path)" Note over L: cwd silently drifted
into one worktree L->>W: five merges - each
prints 'OK' Note over W: all five land on the
worktree's branch L->>M: git rev-parse HEAD M-->>L: still the pre-wave commit Note over L: clean descendant -
recovery is a fast-forward
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain I.C — policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle: integrating parallel work is a lifecycle procedure that needs its own verification step. It exemplifies the verification half of TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) under the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MEASURE function — confirm the change met its specification rather than accept an activity signal — and ISO/IEC 42001's Annex A control themes on the AI system life cycle: change lands with evidence.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
aigp_frameworks: ISO/IEC 42001 — Annex A life-cycle control themes
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Success echoes prove activity, not placement; verify the postcondition from the system of record before trusting multi-step change.

lessons.title: To land on a branch others are actively pushing to, use an operation that can only fast-forward — it refuses rather than races.
lessons.failure: Landing the web-search work, the shared `main` branch would not hold still: it advanced three times mid-merge (`9e693be → e2fe1bd → 3fbc4d7`), each time moments after 'nothing else is running' had been said in good faith. A plain merge would have raced the moving target, and a drifted working directory had already once landed five merges on the wrong branch. The guard was `git merge --ff-only` — an operation that can only fast-forward, advancing `main` onto a commit that already contains it, and refusing otherwise. When `main` moved, the fast-forward declined; the new `main` was re-integrated — the only conflict, every single time, was the journal, resolved by keeping both sides and renumbering the incoming lesson, 36 to 39 to 40 — and the land retried. Bounded and deterministic: a moving target can never produce a wrong land.
evidence: 2026-06-05 — The main that wouldn't hold still, and the merge that refused to race
commits 768346a, a3e18f7, 0fd859a
lessons.lesson: To land on a branch others are actively advancing, use an operation that structurally cannot be wrong about the race — one that refuses rather than guesses. And treat 'nothing is running' as a claim to verify against the repository's own HEAD by absolute path, not a fact to trust: a verbal all-clear and a parallel commit are simply not synchronized.
lessons.growth: The mechanism is the check-then-act gap: between reading the shared state and acting on it, another actor can move it, and no amount of asking more carefully closes that window. The durable fix is choosing primitives whose failure mode is refusal — fast-forward-only merges, compare-and-swap updates, optimistic locking with retry — so a race becomes a clean, bounded retry instead of a silent corruption. Train yourself to spot the shape: anywhere you check a shared thing and then change it (a branch, a record, a config, a booking), ask what happens if it moves between your two steps. If the answer is 'something wrong lands quietly,' replace the careful human with an operation that detects the conflict for you.
lessons.growth_question: If the shared branch moves in the instant between your check and your merge, which protects you: a more careful human, or an operation that refuses?
lessons.diagram:
sequenceDiagram participant S as "Landing session" participant M as "Shared main" participant P as "Parallel sessions" S->>M: read HEAD - 9e693be P->>M: commit lands - main moves S->>M: git merge --ff-only M-->>S: refuses - not a descendant S->>S: re-integrate new main
(only conflict: the journal) S->>M: retry ff-only - lands clean Note over M: a moving target can never
produce a wrong land
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain I.C — policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle: change procedures are strongest when the procedure enforces itself. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's GOVERN function — policy embedded as mechanism rather than reliance on individual care — and ISO/IEC 42001's plan-do-check-act management-system pattern: an observed race fed a structural improvement to how change lands.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Land concurrent change with operations that refuse on conflict; verbal all-clears are never synchronized with shared state.

lessons.title: A coordination discipline that works for one actor becomes the bottleneck when many run at once — scale the mechanism, not just the rule.
lessons.failure: With a security session, a substrate session, and a web-search session each running in its own worktree, `BUILD_JOURNAL.md` — the single file the journal discipline appends to on every substantive change — became a shared-append hotspot git cannot auto-merge. It bit three times in one day: the five-branch Tier-1 wave, the web-search branch's merge of `main`, and a spec-decode commit landing a lesson on top. The rule that would have prevented it already existed for sub-agents — 'report your entry text, don't touch the journal' — but was never given to sessions, so the collision simply moved up a layer. The sharpest contention was lesson numbers: two sessions both reaching for '39.' The fix stopped sharing the file: a per-session fragment inbox, `docs/journal_fragments/`, one file per entry, folded into the canonical journal serially by one integrator, with numbers assigned only at fold-in.
evidence: 2026-06-05 — The journal that conflicted with itself, three times
commit 9a01fc2
lessons.lesson: When a single-actor discipline must scale to many concurrent actors, the medium it rides on has to scale with it, or the discipline becomes the friction it was meant to prevent. Stop sharing the contended file: give each actor an exclusive inbox, serialize the fold at a quiet point, and assign the most contended state only at the serial step.
lessons.growth: The discipline was sound; its substrate — one append-only file — had a concurrency ceiling of one actor, and the jam was structural, not behavioral. You will meet this shape constantly: the sign-off spreadsheet, the shared changelog, the single approval queue — all fine at one actor, all contention at N. The general fix is partition-then-merge: per-actor exclusive resources, one serialized integrator, and the scarcest shared state (here, the running lesson numbers) allocated only at the serial point so parallel actors never race for it. The second, subtler skill: when you patch a coordination rule at one layer, ask which other layers the same collision can climb to — a fix scoped to sub-agents left sessions to rediscover the identical jam.
lessons.growth_question: The journal rule was already fixed for sub-agents — why did the identical collision bite three times in one day, one layer up?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["security session"] --> J["BUILD_JOURNAL.md tail
(one shared append file)"] B["substrate session"] --> J C["web-search session"] --> J J --> X["three collisions in a day;
two sessions reach for '39'"] X --> F["fix: docs/journal_fragments/
one file per entry"] F --> I["one integrator folds
serially at a quiet point"] I --> N["lesson numbers assigned
only at fold-in"]
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain I.C — policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle: procedures must be designed for the scale at which they will actually operate. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's GOVERN function — processes that hold as the organization grows — and ISO/IEC 42001's continual-improvement, plan-do-check-act pattern: an observed process failure produced a structural redesign of the mechanism, not a reminder to try harder.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Scale the mechanism a discipline rides on, not just the rule; shared-append state caps coordination at one actor.

lessons.title: The worktree-isolation quirk can switch the main checkout's branch, so `git -C <main-path>` is not enough — verify the branch before every main-tree commit.
lessons.failure: Launching background worktree builders did not merely move the shell's working directory — it switched the main checkout itself (`C:/Users/mrbla/BlarAI`) onto an agent's feature branch. So a commit made with an explicit `git -C <main-path>` — believed safe precisely because the path was pinned — landed on the feature branch; `main` never advanced; and it was reported as 'on main.' The path and the branch are independent facts: a directory can be the main worktree while its HEAD sits on a feature branch. Recovery stayed inside the never-discard rule, refined by the operator into something cleaner: preserve the stray staged reversal on a throwaway branch (`14d8a7b` on `wip/s14-ea4-staged-reversal-artifact`), switch the checkout back to `main`, cherry-pick the real commit (`4e58a8b`) — no reset, no force, nothing thrown away. The branch-guard added immediately afterward caught the very next recurrence and blocked a wrong-branch merge.
evidence: 2026-06-05 — Locking the door the sessions come through, and the two gremlins at the gate
commits 4e58a8b (cherry-picked recovery), 14d8a7b (preserved reversal)
lessons.lesson: Pinning the path does not pin the branch: verify `git branch --show-current == main` before every commit or merge on the main tree, because the checkout's HEAD can be switched under you by tooling you did not watch. And recover non-destructively — preserve stray state on a throwaway branch, switch back, cherry-pick — so the incident leaves evidence, not wreckage.
lessons.growth: The deeper failure is guard design: the explicit `-C` path was a guard built against the previous incident — a drifting working directory — and it verified one variable while the environment mutated a different one. A safety argument must name the invariant it protects ('commits land on main'), not the last incident it answers, or each recurrence arrives through the unverified variable. In any environment where tools mutate your context behind you (build agents, background jobs, IDE integrations), re-read the full precondition set at the point of action, every time, cheaply — a one-line check beats a post-hoc forensic. And only call a guard real after you have watched it catch a genuine recurrence; this one did, on the very next merge.
lessons.growth_question: You pinned the directory with `git -C` — which independent fact about that directory's state did the pin leave completely unverified?
lessons.diagram:
sequenceDiagram participant W as "Worktree-builder launch" participant M as "Main checkout (the path)" participant S as "Session" W->>M: switches HEAD onto the
EA-4 feature branch S->>M: git -C main-path commit
(path pinned, felt safe) Note over M: commit lands on the
feature branch - main
never advances S->>S: reports it as 'on main' S->>M: recover: wip/ branch,
switch back, cherry-pick Note over M: 4e58a8b on main -
nothing discarded
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain I.C — policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle: a recurring operational hazard answered with a standing checkpoint at the point of action, and an incident recovery that preserved all state for audit. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MANAGE function — risk response plus residual-risk monitoring: the treatment was installed, then observed to catch the next recurrence, which is what demonstrates a treatment works.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Path and branch are independent facts; guard the invariant at the action point, and trust guards only after they catch.

lessons.title: A side effect placed before the point a process can still fork-and-exit runs more than once.
lessons.failure: The launcher self-elevates through a UAC (User Account Control) prompt: the original un-elevated process fires a relaunch, returns 0, and an elevated child carries on. The per-boot certificate mint — a disk-write side effect that issues fresh mutual-TLS certificates each boot — was placed after the dev-mode interlock but before the elevation block, on a reading of 'after the interlock, before service construction' as one contiguous region. It is not: the elevation's return-0 cuts that span in two, so both processes would run the mint — a double-mint, with the short-lived non-elevated process writing the certificates first. The merge-gate review caught the placement before it shipped. The fix moved the mint to a 'Step 1.5' after `admin_ok` is recorded and before the VM starts, so it fires exactly once, in the elevated process.
evidence: 2026-06-06 — The door that was built but never had a lock
commits bcda777, 3fa2315; merged 336fcc2
lessons.lesson: In a startup sequence that can fork and exit — a UAC relaunch is effectively a fork — the position of a side-effecting step relative to the fork point is load-bearing. Side effects belong after the last point the process can still bail out and hand off to a successor, not merely somewhere in the pre-service span.
lessons.growth: Reading startup code linearly hides that control flow can split: 'after X' and 'before Y' can both be true on both sides of a fork, so a step that looks placed once actually executes per process. Map the points where your process can relaunch, re-exec, retry, or be restarted by a supervisor — every one is a multiplier on any side effect upstream of it, and an innocent-looking helper (mint, write, register, send) is still a side effect. Either place effects after the final exit point or make them genuinely idempotent and prove it. Note also what caught this: an independent reviewer reading for placement rather than for the step's own correctness — a second pair of eyes asking a different question than the author asked.
lessons.growth_question: Your startup step runs 'once at boot' — how many times does that span execute when the process relaunches itself elevated halfway through it?
lessons.diagram:
sequenceDiagram participant U as "Un-elevated process" participant E as "Elevated process" Note over U: dev-mode interlock passes U->>U: mint certs - side effect
placed before elevation Note over U: short-lived process has
written the certs first U->>E: UAC relaunch fires Note over U: returns 0 and exits E->>E: re-runs the same span:
mints AGAIN (double-mint) Note over E: fix: mint at Step 1.5,
after admin_ok is recorded
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain III.A — govern the designing and building of the AI system: where security material is generated in the process lifecycle is a design property, and independent review caught it pre-release. It exemplifies TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) under the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MEASURE function — the merge gate as a verification activity — and the secure-and-resilient characteristic: duplicate key material written by the wrong process is an integrity hazard.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
aigp_frameworks: NIST AI RMF — secure and resilient (trustworthy characteristic)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Side effects upstream of a fork-and-exit point run in every process; place them after the last handoff.

lessons.title: When you fix a defect of a particular shape, scan the codebase for the same shape before you close — finding one and fixing only one is a half-fix.
lessons.failure: The session store and the substrate store were built in parallel under the same encryption ADR (Architecture Decision Record), with the same bulk-read pattern — and therefore the same defect: one undecryptable row aborts the entire bulk loop. The session-store fix landed — quarantine the bad row, keep serving the healthy ones — and should have triggered an immediate check of its sibling. It did not. The substrate store sat with the identical fault at two sites: `_load_embed_cache`, which runs at construction time, where a single practice-era row's decrypt failure would stop the store from opening and the Assistant Orchestrator from starting at all; and `_search_kind`, where one bad hit aborts the whole retrieval. The class-audit caught it one boot away from the identical startup brick; the fix was the same shape, applied to the sibling, with the brick-locking test rewritten to assert the quarantine instead.
evidence: 2026-06-06 — When the fix was already written — applying it to the sibling store
commit 3f61b41, merged 6fe1fcc
lessons.lesson: Two components built from the same template carry the same design defects, so when you fix a defect of a particular shape, scan the codebase for the same shape before you close. Finding one and fixing only one is a half-fix; the reflex that closes the class, not the instance, is what stops the sibling biting in production next.
lessons.growth: Defects travel through reuse channels — copied patterns, shared design records, parallel builds from one specification — so a bug found in one instance is evidence about a population, not a point event. At fix time, ask: what else was built from this template, under this decision, by this process, at the same time? Then audit those sites inside the same change, while the defect's shape is sharp in your mind — one grep and an afternoon when systematic, a production outage per sibling when skipped. Make the class-audit part of your definition of 'done' for any defect fix, and notice the special trap here: a passing test had even locked the sibling's wrong behavior, so the audit must re-examine the tests too.
lessons.growth_question: You just fixed a defect born from a shared pattern — what does that finding tell you about every other component built from the same pattern?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["one encryption ADR,
one bulk-read pattern"] --> B["session store"] A --> C["substrate store
(the sibling)"] B --> D["defect found and fixed:
quarantine bad rows"] C --> E["same defect, untouched,
at two sites"] E --> F["_load_embed_cache: one bad
row bricks AO startup"] E --> G["_search_kind: one bad hit
aborts the whole retrieve"] D -.-> H["class-audit: apply the
written fix to the sibling"] E -.-> H
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain III.C — govern the release, monitoring and maintenance of the model and system: corrective action in a managed system extends to the class of nonconformity, not the single instance. It exemplifies ISO/IEC 42001's continual-improvement, plan-do-check-act pattern — the check found a defect, the act covered its siblings — and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MANAGE function: treat the risk wherever it recurs.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
lessons.aigp_takeaway: A defect with a shape indicts every sibling built from the same template; close the class before closing the ticket.

lessons.title: A long-"deferred" code path is where bugs hide from each other — activating it is a discovery exercise, not a one-liner.
lessons.failure: The #615 guest boundary rode through several sprints as one deferred line item — 'Windows AF_HYPERV addressing fix' — preserved but never exercised. Activating it revealed the single label had hidden two opposite defects that masked each other because nothing in the path had ever run. The shared transport had the right protocol but built a Linux-style `(cid, port)` address, where Windows Hyper-V sockets address by a `(VmId, ServiceId)` pair of GUID strings — the host literally cannot reach a guest with the integer form. The gateway had the right GUID address but omitted the protocol argument, raising `WSAEPROTOTYPE` (WinError 10041) before its address was ever used. Neither had ever thrown. The oracle that settled the correct form was not documentation or memory but `phase2_gates/evidence/vsock_validation.json` — the February validation run on the real hardware, recording the working topology. The evidence file was the spec.
evidence: 2026-06-07 — The two bugs that hid behind one "deferred" label
lessons.lesson: When you finally exercise dormant code, budget for plural defects and find the empirical oracle — the artifact recording the configuration that actually worked — rather than trusting in-code comments that describe intent, not working form. Sibling to 'committed is not done until verified': dormant is not correct until exercised.
lessons.growth: Execution is the only force that surfaces a whole class of faults, so a path that never runs accumulates them invisibly — and multiple faults along one dead path produce zero signal, because the first failure never happens in a way that would expose the second. Deferral also rots descriptions: comments record what the author intended at write time, not what ever worked. So treat 'activate the deferred thing' as a discovery exercise — scaffold tests around it, budget time for several faults, and expect the bugs to be of opposite kinds. Before reviving anything dormant, hunt for the empirical record — a validation log, a known-good configuration, a captured working run — and promote it to the specification, because it is the only description guaranteed to have once been true.
lessons.growth_question: If a code path has never once executed, how many faults could it contain — and which of its comments has ever been tested against reality?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["one 'deferred' label,
carried for sprints,
never exercised"] --> B["shared transport path"] A --> C["gateway path"] B --> D["right protocol, wrong
address: Linux-style
(cid, port) integers"] C --> E["right GUID address, no
protocol arg: raises
WinError 10041 first"] D --> F["dormant - neither defect
ever threw; each masked
the other"] E --> F F --> G["oracle: February's
vsock_validation.json"] G --> H["(VmId, ServiceId) GUID
pair, HV_PROTOCOL_RAW"]
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain III.C — govern the release, monitoring and maintenance of the model and system: reactivating long-dormant functionality is a change that needs its own verification cycle. It exemplifies TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) under the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MEASURE function, including its verification-versus-validation distinction: the comments described the intended build, while the archived evidence artifact recorded the validated working form — only the latter could serve as the oracle.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Dormant code accumulates masked, plural faults; activate it as discovery work against an empirical oracle, never against its comments.

lessons.title: Cross-stream seams that merge separately must connect by registration, not by import.
lessons.failure: Sprint 17 split one egress mechanism across two parallel streams: H-a owned `egress_guard` — the socket-level anchor with the kill-switch — and merged first; H-b owned the `exfil_screen` that plugs into it, merging later. The halves had to connect across that merge boundary without a circular import, since the anchor cannot import a module that imports the anchor. The tempting design had `egress_guard` reach into a known module path; the shipped design inverted it: the screen module imports the anchor and calls `register_screener(...)` and `register_arm_hook(...)`, and the anchor never names its dependent. That let H-a ship and test the entire seam end-to-end with a fake screener driven through the real socket send path before H-b's code existed — the exact signatures frozen in a contract test, and the launcher arming the baseline guard even on a checkout where the screen module is absent.
evidence: 2026-06-07 — Building the door before there is anything to let through
2026-06-07 — Building the screen for a door that is still welded shut
lessons.lesson: When two streams own opposite halves of one mechanism and merge at different times, the module that merges second registers into the anchor; the anchor never imports its dependent. The dependency graph stays acyclic across the merge boundary, and the first stream proves the wiring with a fake before the second stream's code exists.
lessons.growth: Parallel development adds a time dimension to your dependency graph: the anchor must be complete, testable, and shippable while its plug-in is still unmerged, and an import-by-name cannot deliver that — it creates both a cycle and a hard dependency on code that is not there yet. Registration inverts the dependency; it is the same shape underneath plugin systems and dependency injection. When you split one mechanism across streams or teams, design the seam first: freeze the interface in a contract test both sides build against, decide explicitly who merges first, and define what the early side does when the late side is absent — here, fail toward the more restrictive posture, because a missing screen must never become an open door.
lessons.growth_question: Stream A must ship and fully test the guard before stream B's screener exists — in which direction can the dependency between them safely point?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["egress_guard anchor
(stream H-a, merges first)"] B["exfil_screen plug-in
(stream H-b, merges later)"] B --> C["imports the anchor, calls
register_screener and
register_arm_hook"] C --> A A --> D["anchor never names its
dependent - graph acyclic"] A --> E["seam proven end-to-end
with a fake screener"] A --> F["screen absent on a checkout?
baseline guard still arms"] G["rejected: anchor imports
a known module path"] -.-> H["circular import across
the merge boundary"]
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain III.A — govern the designing and building of the AI system under a multi-stream development structure: interface contracts, merge order, and absence behavior are design governance. It exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's GOVERN function — processes that let independent workstreams compose safely — and its secure-and-resilient trustworthy characteristic: a missing or failing screener degrades to the more restrictive posture, never to an open door.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
aigp_frameworks: NIST AI RMF — secure and resilient (trustworthy characteristic)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Across merge boundaries, late modules register into early anchors — never the reverse — and absence fails toward the restrictive posture.

lessons.title: Put a carve-out where the rule actually fires, not where its name says it lives.
lessons.failure: The brief and the SDV (the sprint's signed design-and-validation spec) both located the egress deny in the Policy Agent's `rule_engine` — and a `services/policy_agent/src/rule_engine.py` genuinely exists. But `DENY_EXTERNAL_NETWORK` is not in it. The deterministic deny is RULE 3 of `DeterministicPolicyChecker.check()` in `gpu_inference.py` — a single classmethod enforced at both the Policy Agent boundary and the Assistant Orchestrator's tool loop, so the assistant's own tool calls get the same deny. Put the allowlist carve-out where the filename pointed and it would have either missed the AO path entirely or forked the rule into two copies that drift — the more dangerous outcome in a security control. The carve-out went where the rule fires, behind a keyword-only parameter defaulting to an empty frozenset, with a dormancy lock that fails loudly if that default is ever populated without the gate.
evidence: 2026-06-07 — Building the screen for a door that is still welded shut
lessons.lesson: Verify the enforcement point on disk before trusting the module name in the brief: a filename is documentation, the call graph is the truth. Amending the real chokepoint covers every call site with one change; amending where the name points either misses a path or forks the rule into copies that drift apart.
lessons.growth: Names are claims, and claims age: code organization drifts away from its labels as a system grows, while inherited documents repeat the stale claim with full confidence — each author trusting the last. For any policy-bearing change, trace from the behavior backward: search for the rule's actual identifier, follow the call graph to where the decision is made, enumerate every caller, and only then choose where the change lands. A rule with one enforcement point is auditable; the same rule in two places is two rules, and they will eventually disagree — silently, in whichever path gets less traffic. The five-minute trace is always cheaper than the fork, and it is the difference between amending a policy and quietly splitting it.
lessons.growth_question: The design document names the module that owns the rule — what is the difference between where a rule is said to live and where it actually fires?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["brief and SDV point at
rule_engine.py"] --> B["the file exists - but the
deny is not in it"] C["DENY_EXTERNAL_NETWORK =
RULE 3 in gpu_inference.py"] --> D["enforced at the
PA boundary"] C --> E["enforced in the AO
tool loop (same classmethod)"] B -.-> F["carve-out at the filename:
misses the AO path or
forks the rule"] C --> G["carve-out at RULE 3:
one change, every call site"]
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain I.C — establish policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle: a policy rule needs a single, verified enforcement point, or amendments fragment it into drifting copies. It exemplifies, at concept level, the EU AI Act's technical-documentation obligation — documentation must match the system as built — and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's GOVERN function: policy is only effective at the point where it is actually enforced.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
aigp_frameworks: EU AI Act — technical documentation (concept level)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Filenames are claims; the call graph is truth. Amend a rule only at its single, verified enforcement point.

lessons.title: A frozen-dataclass exception type masks an unexpected raise behind a confusing secondary error.
lessons.failure: Building the dynamic production-posture guard, the first dev-mode control test blew up not with a clean assertion failure but with `dataclasses.FrozenInstanceError: cannot assign to field '__traceback__'`. The true cause sat behind that message: the test's temporary dev config omitted `[gpu].priority`, which the validator requires even in dev mode, so config loading raised a `ConfigResolutionError` the test was not expecting — a stricter-than-expected validator catching a thin fixture, which is a feature, not a fault. But `ConfigResolutionError` is a frozen dataclass (a Python value object made immutable after construction) that also subclasses `ValueError`: when the unexpected exception propagated through pytest's `with`-block machinery, the attempt to attach a traceback hit the frozen wall, and the secondary error masked the real message. Completing the fixture config fixed it cleanly; all twelve gate-tier assertions then executed with zero skips.
evidence: 2026-06-07 — The posture the file swears to versus the posture the boot resolves
lessons.lesson: When `FrozenInstanceError: cannot assign to field '__traceback__'` appears, the real failure is an unexpected exception of a frozen type escaping the test machinery. Read the wrapped exception's value instead of chasing the traceback assignment — and make control fixtures complete rather than minimal, because a strict validator rejecting a thin fixture is doing its job.
lessons.growth: Two individually reasonable choices — immutable exception objects, and tooling that annotates exceptions as they fly — composed into a misleading signature: the error you saw was the collision, not the cause. Every stack has masking signatures like this; the skill is learning to recognize when a failure message names infrastructure (tracebacks, serialization, logging, pickling) rather than your domain, and treating that as a strong hint that a different exception is hiding underneath. Then there is the fixture half: a test fixture meant as a control must satisfy the system's full validity contract, or the thing you are accidentally testing becomes the validator's strictness instead of the behavior you meant to isolate. Minimal fixtures are a false economy under strict validation.
lessons.growth_question: The test died on 'cannot assign to field __traceback__' — is that the bug itself, or the wreckage of a different exception trying to surface?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["test's dev config omits
gpu.priority"] --> B["validator raises
ConfigResolutionError
(unexpected by the test)"] B --> C["raise flies through
pytest's with-block
machinery"] C --> D["pytest attaches
__traceback__ to it"] D --> E["frozen dataclass refuses:
FrozenInstanceError"] E --> F["secondary error masks
the real message"] F --> G["fix: complete the fixture;
read the wrapped exception"]
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain III.A — govern the designing and building of the AI system: how exception types are designed determines how diagnosable failures are, and verification depends on failures that report their true cause. It exemplifies TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) under the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MEASURE function — correctly interpreting test evidence is itself part of the verification activity, and a masked raise aims the investigation at the wrong layer.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Infrastructure-flavored errors often mask the real raise; read the wrapped exception, and keep control fixtures contract-complete.

lessons.title: Reference paths are for reading; writes go to the worktree — verify the file landed where the branch lives, not just that the branch is right.
lessons.failure: Sprint 18's stream M built two production-posture locks inside an isolated worktree — and nearly lost the work to a directory nobody was watching. The brief's reference paths pointed at the main checkout, correct for reading context; that path leaked into the writes, and both new files — the C1 harness and the C2 edit — landed in the main checkout, the one tree the stream was explicitly told not to touch. Running test collection against the worktree surfaced it: the files were not where the branch lived. Recovery followed the non-destructive discipline: copy both files into the worktree, SHA-verify the copies byte-identical (a SHA is a cryptographic fingerprint of a file's exact contents), preserve a backup, and only then restore main to pristine — with a pre-existing change in main that was not stream M's left strictly untouched.
evidence: 2026-06-08 — The two ways `dev_mode` lies, and the worktree that wasn't where I wrote
commits e51119d (merged b1d7c96), e3dbee0 (merged dedf341)
lessons.lesson: The worktree hazard is not only the branch: absolute paths that are correct for reading silently become write targets. A `pwd`/`ls` of the file you just wrote, checked against the worktree root, catches it before commit — the filesystem-path counterpart of the verify-the-branch guard from lesson 52.
lessons.growth: Context documents mix two kinds of paths — read references into the canonical tree, and write targets inside your isolated workspace — and nothing in the path string itself marks which is which, so under load the distinction collapses toward whatever is written down. In any isolated-workspace model (worktrees, sandboxes, staging environments), add a cheap landing check: after creating each artifact, verify it sits inside the isolation boundary before you commit anything. And point your verification machinery at the workspace, not the canonical tree — here it was collection run against the worktree that surfaced the escape, precisely because the files were missing where they should have been. A check aimed at the wrong tree passes for the wrong reason and tells you nothing.
lessons.growth_question: Your brief lists absolute paths into the main checkout so you can read context — what stops those same strings from becoming the destination of your next write?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["brief: absolute reference
paths into the main checkout"] --> B["correct for READING context"] A --> C["the same strings leak
into the WRITES"] C --> D["C1 harness + C2 edit land
in the main checkout"] E["test collection run
against the worktree"] --> F["files missing where
the branch lives"] D --> F F --> G["copy into the worktree,
SHA-verify byte-identical"] G --> H["only then restore
main to pristine"]
lessons.aigp_text: This maps to AIGP Domain I.C — policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle: workspace isolation for parallel work holds only when both the branch and the write path are verified, and the recovery preserved byte-verified evidence of every artifact. It exemplifies TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation) under the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MEASURE function — collection run against the intended tree was the verification that surfaced the escape — alongside GOVERN's accountability for working inside a declared boundary.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Reading paths and writing paths are different permissions; verify every artifact landed inside the isolation boundary before commit.

lessons.title: When a read contradicts the premise of your task, the read wins — pause before you write.
lessons.failure: Three follow-ups came back from the Lead Architect's #612 deep-dive, among them a sweep of residual-card ticket references to live numbers. Starting on the sweep, a routine grep — a text search across files — returned the new numbers instead of the old. The sweep was already done. So were the diagram fix and a new Policy-Agent card, none by this session: a concurrent session was editing the same make_outline.py in the main tree, uncommitted, a live preview server running on another port. Without that grep, the session would have re-applied the sweep on top of another session's uncommitted work, bundled their changes into its commit, and possibly raced them into a lost edit — the exact failure this project has paid for before. Instead: stop, surface what was found, ask how to coordinate. The Lead Architect chose take-over; the concurrent work was verified on disk and committed cleanly (b08a45f).
evidence: 2026-06-09 — The grep that caught a parallel session, and the gap that became a decision
commit b08a45f
lessons.lesson: A grep run to do a task instead revealed the task was already done by a parallel session; proceeding on the original premise would have collided on shared state. When a read contradicts the premise of your task, the read wins — treat the contradicting result as a stop signal, surface it, and confirm before mutating anything.
lessons.growth: A task brief is a snapshot of the world at assignment time; on shared, concurrent state, the world can move between briefing and execution, and your first read of the actual state is fresher evidence than the brief. The failure mode is premise inertia: the plan is already loaded, so contradicting evidence gets explained away as noise and the write proceeds. Train the opposite reflex — any read that contradicts your task's premise is a checkpoint: stop writing, inventory what you actually see, surface it, and re-confirm the task against reality. This generalizes to every shared workspace: documents, tickets, configuration, databases, calendars. It pairs with lesson 41's moving-main discipline (verify 'nothing is running' against the repository itself) and lesson 48 (a load-bearing premise gets verified on disk, not inherited). Reading before writing is the cheapest insurance there is; surprise is signal, never noise.
lessons.growth_question: Your first command toward a task returns evidence the task is already done — what does writing anyway put at risk, and whose work is it?
lessons.diagram:
sequenceDiagram participant C as "Concurrent session" participant T as "Main tree (shared)" participant S as "This session" Note over S: task: sweep ticket refs
to live numbers C->>T: edits make_outline.py -
uncommitted, preview live S->>T: routine grep for the
old shorthand tickets T-->>S: returns the NEW numbers Note over S: premise dead - the sweep
is already done S->>S: stop, surface, ask
how to coordinate Note over T: LA chose take-over - work
verified, committed b08a45f
lessons.aigp_text: NIST AI RMF's GOVERN function is the cross-cutting one — risk culture, policies, accountability — and 'pause and surface when evidence contradicts the premise' is risk culture operating at the level of a single command: it is what keeps two actors from silently corrupting shared state. ISO/IEC 42001's management-system pattern places check before act for the same reason. The coordinated stop-surface-confirm exemplifies Domain I.C, policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
lessons.aigp_takeaway: The freshest read of shared state outranks the task brief; treat surprise as a stop signal and confirm before you write.

lessons.title: Parallel sessions that each author "Amendment N" to the same ADR collide silently
lessons.failure: The UC-002/003 program landed on main as four merges, ~11,300 inserted lines; three merged conflict-free, the fourth conflicted only in two governance documents. Two parallel sessions had each authored 'Amendment 1' to ADR-027, the Architecture Decision Record carrying the canonical egress policy: one session wrote Am.1 as the mechanics (the one-door guarded_fetch seam), the other as the policy (per-action INGEST_FETCH adjudication, paste-is-the-consent). Same number, same date, two halves of one decision, written blind to each other. Git surfaced the Status line as a conflict but happily placed both full sections fifty lines apart — two competing 'Amendment 1's in the canonical egress policy. The integrator unified them into a single record — policy first, mechanics as its implementing subsections — and rejected renumbering one limb to 'Amendment 2': both were ratified as Amendment 1 and already cited that way in the register and commit history; renumbering would have falsified provenance.
evidence: 2026-06-10 — Two sessions wrote the same amendment, and the merge had to make them one law
commits e8aba5c, bf517ac
lessons.lesson: Identifiers assigned at authorship time collide when authorship is parallel: two ratified 'Amendment 1's, same number, disjoint content. Like the journal's lesson numbers, amendment numbers want assignment at integration time. Until that discipline exists, the integrator must expect to unify competing same-numbered records into one — documented in the register — and never renumber a ratified limb, because existing citations make the old number part of the truth.
lessons.growth: Any serial counter is a hidden piece of shared state: two writers who cannot see each other will both take 'the next number,' and a text-merge tool will not save you, because it resolves line collisions, not semantic ones — git was satisfied the moment both sections fit in the file. The defenses are structural. Allocate serial identifiers at a single integration point — this project already does it for journal lesson numbers, where fragments are 'described, never pre-numbered' — or use collision-free identifiers (dates, slugs) at authorship and map them to numbers at fold-in. Watch for the same shape in ticket numbers, schema migration versions, and API version tags. And when a collision has already been ratified into citations, repair by unification, never renumbering: the references are part of the historical record. A green merge proves the lines fit; only a semantic pass proves the law is one law.
lessons.growth_question: Two writers who cannot see each other both need 'the next number' — why is the resulting collision invisible to the merge tool, and at what single point should the number be assigned instead?
lessons.aigp_text: Governance runs on records that are single and authoritative, and two ratified amendments sharing one number is a record-integrity failure no tooling flagged. The EU AI Act's record-keeping concept and NIST AI RMF's GOVERN function — policies, accountability structures — both assume the record can be traced; unifying the limbs on the record while refusing to rewrite ratified citations preserves that traceability. The discipline exemplifies Domain I.C: policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle, applied to the decision log itself.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Serial identifiers are shared state: assign them at integration, and repair ratified collisions by unification on the record, never renumbering.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["egress-door session
writes Amendment 1:
mechanics (guarded_fetch)"] --> C["git merge of ADR-027"] B["UC-003 session
writes Amendment 1:
policy (INGEST_FETCH)"] --> C C --> D["conflict shown: one
Status line only"] C --> E["both sections placed
fifty lines apart"] E --> F["two ratified 'Amendment 1's
in the canonical policy"] F --> G["unify into one record;
document in the register"] F -.-> H["rejected: renumber one
limb (falsifies provenance)"]

lessons.title: An ownership-conditional cleanup ("only release what I acquired") is a ratchet whenever the ownership flag can latch off from a transient event
lessons.failure: The Lead Architect's report was concrete and a little maddening: close BlarAI cleanly, and the 2 GiB BlarAI-Orchestrator Hyper-V VM (virtual machine) stays Running — and the next clean exit does not fix it either. The root cause was an ownership ratchet in the launcher's cleanup. At startup a flag recorded 'I only own the VM if I was the one who turned it on' (_vm_was_started), and cleanup stopped the VM only if that flag held. But the flag is sticky in the wrong direction: the moment the VM is ever left running across a launcher start — a crash, a hard console close, a host reboot — the next launch sees Running, sets the flag False, and every clean exit thereafter skips the stop. One transient event leaks the VM forever. The day's launcher.log showed the exact signature: 'stopping Policy Agent service', then 'Cleanup: complete', with no 'stopping Hyper-V VM' line between them.
evidence: 2026-06-10 — The VM that owned itself out of being stopped
lessons.lesson: An ownership-conditional cleanup — 'only release what I acquired' — is a ratchet whenever the ownership flag can latch off from a transient event: once it does, the resource leaks forever while every subsequent clean run looks correct. Decide teardown from the resource's current observable state ('is it running now?'), keep the provenance ('did I start it?') mode only as an explicit opt-in for genuine shared ownership, and make the unrecognised or default path fall to release, never retain.
lessons.growth: The flag is a cached belief about history, computed once at startup, and a cleanup keyed on it inherits every failure of every run before it: a crash skips the stop, the next start observes 'already running,' concludes 'not mine,' and the belief never repairs — the leak re-arms itself on each clean exit. A decision keyed on current observable state self-heals instead, because each run re-asks the question the resource can actually answer. Watch for the pattern wherever 'did I create this?' guards 'should I release this?' — temporary files, lock files, containers, cloud resources — and prefer 'does it exist now, and should it?' Keep the provenance mode only as explicit opt-in for true shared ownership, and route unrecognised configuration to release, never retain, so a typo cannot re-arm the ratchet. The detection signature is cheap and durable: a teardown log that completes without the release line it should contain.
lessons.growth_question: The cleanup ran, the log reads 'complete', and the 2 GiB VM is still running — what did the teardown condition consult, and when was that answer last true?
lessons.aigp_text: ISO/IEC 42001's management-system pattern — plan-do-check-act — places a check against observed reality at the heart of continual improvement, and this fix is that idea at code scale: teardown now consults the resource's live state instead of a remembered intention. Among NIST AI RMF's trustworthy-AI characteristics, 'valid and reliable' covers this directly — a system that releases what it holds, run after run, under faults. Pairing the fix with a runbook and a log signature to watch exemplifies Domain III.C — governing the release, monitoring and maintenance of the system.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
aigp_frameworks: NIST AI RMF — trustworthy characteristics (valid and reliable)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Tear down from observed current state, not remembered ownership; a latched flag leaks the resource forever while every log looks clean.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["boot 1: VM was Off
flag: I started it"] --> B["transient event: crash,
hard close, host reboot"] B --> C["VM left Running
2 GiB still held"] C --> D["boot 2 sees Running:
flag latches to not-mine"] D --> E["every later clean exit
skips Stop-VM"] E --> C F["StartIfRunning re-inflates
the leaked VM each boot"] --> C G["fix: stop whenever live
state is Running"] -.-> E
